straight_beat.name: Hal Cifuentes
straight_beat.column: The Angels won yesterday, 7-1, but you could feel the weight of the loss in the air. Not the loss, mind you—this was a win, a hard-earned one, with the kind of resilience that makes you want to believe in the team again. But here’s the box score talking: they’re 9-9,

optimist.name: Sunny Escalante
optimist.column:
The Angels didn’t just beat the Yankees yesterday—they flipped the script, flipped the field, and flipped the script again. A 7-1 drubbing? Sure, but let’s not forget the way Rengifo flipped that double play in the third. That’s October magic hiding in April, and it’s a sign the team’s got more than just a .500 record on its hands.  

You know the drill: the AL West is a beast, and the Angels are still finding their claws. But here’s the thing—this isn’t a team that’s been handed a playbook. They’re the kind of squad that thrives on chaos, the kind that turns a 9-1 loss into a lesson and a 7-1 win into a rallying cry. Yesterday’s game was a reminder that even in a division as stacked as this, the Angels have the grit to make their mark.  

Let’s talk about the bullpen. Those guys? They’re the unsung heroes, the ones who keep the rallies alive when the starters fade. Yesterday, they didn’t just hold serve—they turned

pessimist.name: Murray Doblinski
pessimist.column:
The Angels won yesterday. That’s the first thing you’ll read. The second thing is they’re still 9-9. The third thing is the AL West is a meat grinder, and the fourth thing is Arte Moreno is probably sipping a Cabernet somewhere, smug as a man who bought a lottery ticket and won a donut.  

Let’s not kid ourselves. A 7-1 romp over the Yankees isn’t a sign of progress. It’s a reminder of how easily this team can turn a game into a carnival. They’re scoring runs, sure—nine of them, in fact—but nine runs against a team that’s been struggling to score? That’s not a blueprint. That’s a one-night
